an3.30:1.1: “Monks, there are these three types of persons to be found existing in the world.
an3.30:1.3: The person of upside down discernment, the person of lap discernment, and the person of wide-open discernment.
an3.30:1.4: “And which is the person of upside-down discernment?
an3.30:1.5: There is the case where a person, having gone to a monastery, often listens to the Dhamma in the presence of the monks.
an3.30:1.6: The monks teach him the Dhamma admirable in the beginning, admirable in the middle, admirable in the end. They expound the holy life both in its particulars & in its meaning, entirely perfect, surpassingly pure.
an3.30:1.7: He, while sitting in that seat, doesn’t attend to the beginning of that talk, doesn’t attend to the middle, doesn’t attend to the end.
an3.30:1.8: Having gotten up from that seat, he doesn’t attend to the beginning of that talk, doesn’t attend to the middle, doesn’t attend to the end.
an3.30:1.9: Just as when a pot is turned upside down, water poured there runs off and doesn’t stay;
an3.30:1.10: in the same way, there is the case where a person, having gone to a monastery, often listens to the Dhamma in the presence of the monks.
an3.30:1.11: The monks teach him the Dhamma admirable in the beginning, admirable in the middle, admirable in the end. They expound the holy life both in its particulars & in its meaning, entirely perfect, surpassingly pure.
an3.30:1.12: He, while sitting in that seat, doesn’t attend to the beginning of that talk, doesn’t attend to the middle, doesn’t attend to the end.
an3.30:1.13: Having gotten up from that seat, he doesn’t attend to the beginning of that talk, doesn’t attend to the middle, doesn’t attend to the end.
an3.30:1.14: This is called a person of upside down discernment.
an3.30:2.1: “And which is the person of lap discernment?
an3.30:2.2: There is the case where a person, having gone to a monastery, often listens to the Dhamma in the presence of the monks.
an3.30:2.3: The monks teach him the Dhamma admirable in the beginning, admirable in the middle, admirable in the end. They expound the holy life both in its particulars & in its meaning, entirely perfect, surpassingly pure.
an3.30:2.4: He, while sitting in that seat, attends to the beginning of that talk, attends to the middle, attends to the end.
an3.30:2.5: But having gotten up from that seat, he doesn’t attend to the beginning of that talk, doesn’t attend to the middle, doesn’t attend to the end.
an3.30:2.6: Just as when a person has various foods strewn over his lap—
an3.30:2.7: sesame seeds, husked rice, cakes, & jujubes—
an3.30:2.8: and when getting up, his mindfulness lapsed, he would scatter them;
an3.30:2.9: in the same way, there is the case where a person, having gone to a monastery, often listens to the Dhamma in the presence of the monks.
an3.30:2.10: The monks teach him the Dhamma admirable in the beginning, admirable in the middle, admirable in the end. They expound the holy life both in its particulars & in its meaning, entirely perfect, surpassingly pure.
an3.30:2.11: He, while sitting in that seat, attends to the beginning of that talk, attends to the middle, attends to the end.
an3.30:2.12: But having gotten up from that seat, he doesn’t attend to the beginning of that talk, doesn’t attend to the middle, doesn’t attend to the end.
an3.30:2.13: This is called a person of lap discernment.
an3.30:3.1: “And which is the person of wide open discernment?
an3.30:3.2: There is the case where a person, having gone to a monastery, often listens to the Dhamma in the presence of the monks.
an3.30:3.3: The monks teach him the Dhamma admirable in the beginning, admirable in the middle, admirable in the end. They expound the holy life both in its particulars & in its meaning, entirely perfect, surpassingly pure.
an3.30:3.4: He, while sitting in that seat, attends to the beginning of that talk, attends to the middle, attends to the end.
an3.30:3.5: And having gotten up from that seat, attends to the beginning of that talk, attends to the middle, attends to the end.
an3.30:3.6: Just as when a pot is set right side up, water poured there stays and doesn’t run off;
an3.30:3.7: in the same way, there is the case where a person, having gone to a monastery, often listens to the Dhamma in the presence of the monks.
an3.30:3.8: The monks teach him the Dhamma admirable in the beginning, admirable in the middle, admirable in the end. They expound the holy life both in its particulars & in its meaning, entirely perfect, surpassingly pure.
an3.30:3.9: He, while sitting in that seat, attends to the beginning of that talk, attends to the middle, attends to the end.
an3.30:3.10: And having gotten up from that seat, attends to the beginning of that talk, attends to the middle, attends to the end.
an3.30:3.11: This is called a person of wide open discernment.”
an3.30:4.1: A man of upside down discernment—
an3.30:4.4: goes in the presence of the monks,
an3.30:5.2: like the beginning, middle, or end
an3.30:5.4: for discernment isn’t found in him.
an3.30:6.2: is said to be better than that one.
an3.30:6.4: in the presence of the monks,
an3.30:7.1: while sitting in that seat, grasps the words of the beginning, middle, & end of the talk,
an3.30:7.5: but getting up, he doesn’t discern anything like that,
an3.30:7.6: for he forgets what he had grasped.
an3.30:8.1: But a man of wide open discernment
an3.30:8.2: is said to be better than those ones.
an3.30:8.4: goes in the presence of the monks,
an3.30:9.1: while sitting in that seat, he grasps the words of the beginning, middle, & end of the talk.
an3.30:10.1: He remembers—the person of undivided mind, with the best of resolves.
an3.30:10.2: Practicing the Dhamma in line with the Dhamma,
